author: Mao, Wenchao; Zhang, Zewu; Yi, Baojun; Zha, Xiaojian; Dong, Jia'ning; Le, Xiaoyu; Chen, Wen; Zhang, Liqi
title: Numerical study on the oxidation/gasification reaction behavior of a single rod-shaped biomass char particle during oxy-fuel combustion: Insights of the enhancement factors modification
abstract_note: In oxy-fuel combustion, elevated CO2 and H2O concentrations enhance char gasification reactions, while variations in the aspect ratio of rod-shaped biomass particles influence oxidation/gasification competition by modifying the surrounding flow fields. However, existing combustion sub-models struggle to accurately capture this competitive behavior. To this end, the present study systematically investigates the oxy-fuel combustion characteristics of single rod-shaped biomass char particles with equivalent diameters (D-eq) ranging from 0.2 to 2 mm and aspect ratios (AR) between 1 and 4.5. Based on these results, a new enhancement factor model is proposed to predict char oxidation and gasification reactions. The flame temperature and both homogenous and heterogeneous reactions intensified at the particle ends due to the combined effects of D-eq and AR. D-eq exerts a more substant influence, particularly within 0.2 similar to 1 mm. Variations in D-eq and AR affect oxidation/gasification competition by altering the convection-diffusion balance. For small particles (D-eq = 0.2 mm), the heterogeneous reactions slightly favor char gasification under combined kinetic-diffusion control. For larger particles, the reactions shift toward kinetic control, and oxidation becomes dominant. Furthermore, the proposed enhancement factor model accurately describes the influence of D-eq and AR on oxidation/gasification competition, exhibiting high fitting performance (R-2 > 0.98, RMSE < 0.01) and good agreement with the simulation results. The proposed enhancement factor model can be readily integrated into existing biomass combustion frameworks, providing as a more accurate predictive tool for designing and optimizing macro-scale biomass oxy-fuel reactors.
keywords: Oxy-fuel combustion; Aspect ratio; Biomass particle; Char oxidation/gasification reaction; Equivalent diameter

author: Hai, Liya; Liu, Gang; Zhang, Jingbo; Li, Xiaoshan; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi; Wu, Fan
title: A provincial-level multi-criteria assessment of hydrogen supply-demand potential in china: integrating industrial demand, policy, resources, and technology
abstract_note: This study provides a thorough approach to evaluating the potential for hydrogen supply and demand across 30 Chinese regions. The framework includes six components: carbon market mechanisms, supply-side resources, demand-side applications, technological capabilities, policy drivers, and environmental restrictions. The framework combines the Analytic Hierarchy Process(AHP) with the PROMETHEE II method to adapt to heterogeneous indicators and capture the non-compensatory trade-off relationships among different dimensions. The analysis reveals significant spatial heterogeneity in China's hydrogen development landscape. Resourcerich northwest provinces such as Xinjiang and Inner Mongolia have abundant renewable energy potential for green hydrogen production, yet they have limited local industrial demand. Meanwhile, Jiangsu and Shandong, two coastal industrial centers, demonstrate high hydrogen consumption, yet face limitations in domestic green hydrogen supply due to limited renewable capacity and land availability. Correlation and sensitivity analyses further indicate that hydrogen development potential is currently more strongly associated with demand-side and technological factors than with resource endowment alone. These results suggest that China's hydrogen transition still relies on the existing industrial and energy system structure, characterized by persistent spatial disconnection between supply and demand, and uneven policy effectiveness. The framework proposed in this paper provides a robust and policy-relevant tool for identifying regional functional positioning and supporting coordinated hydrogen development strategies.
keywords: Hydrogen energy; Multi-criteria decision making (MCDM); PROMETHEE II; Supply-demand coupling

author: Xie, Jiaojiao; Zhi, Xinyu; Lu, Bowen; Hong, Dikun; Zhang, Liqi
title: Fe2O3 aided NiFe2O4 enhanced CO2 hydrogenation to CO
abstract_note: NiFe2O4 has been widely applied in CO2 hydrogenation for its excellent redox properties. However, NiFe2O4 samples usually exhibited complex performance, rapid inactivation followed by slow activation, and poor hydrogenation selectivity. In this work, the complex behavior of NiFe2O4 in CO2 hydrogenation was studied, and the influence of surface oxide species(NiO and Fe2O3) on its catalytic performance was probed on NiFe2O4-ww through magnetic water washing. NiFe2O4-ww was observed to predominantly produce CO with nearly 100% selectivity at lower temperatures and demonstrated a higher CO2 conversion rate of 71.90% at 700 degrees C. However, under high-temperature reducing conditions, NiFe2O4-ww underwent rapid reduction to form Fe-Ni alloy, which induced sintering and carbon deposition, thereby leading to a significant decline in catalytic activity. Surface NiO species on NiFe2O4 enhanced CO2 hydrogenation at lower temperatures but reduced the selectivity towards CO. Surface Fe2O3 species enhanced the catalytic resistance to reduction, thereby improving catalyst stability by inhibiting the accumulation of carbon deposition. The effect of residual oxides on the NiFe2O4 surface may give insight into understanding the deactivation behavior of Fe-Ni alloy in CO2 hydrogenation.
keywords: RWGS; Fe-Ni alloy; Stability; Fe - Ni alloy

author: Xu, Bin; Gao, Xiaoyi; Tang, Xi; Li, Xiaoshan; Luo, Cong; Wu, Fan; Chen, Wen; Zhang, Liqi
title: Incorporating ineffective porosity into mass transfer models for improving prediction of CO2 absorption into packed columns
abstract_note: In gas purification processes, absorption in packed columns is one of the most commonly used and highly efficient methods for gas removal. However, the prediction of mass transfer performance in packed columns often suffers from significant uncertainties, which has long been a critical challenge in industrial design, process analysis, and simulation. To address this issue, the present study introduces the concept of ineffective porosity (su) and systematically evaluates its role in improving the prediction accuracy of mass transfer performance in packed columns. First, the value of su was determined using the Billet inverse calculation method, and the results demonstrated an approximately linear relationship between su and the liquid holdup (H). Subsequently, experiments were conducted to examine the effects of operating parameters on the effective interfacial area (av) and mass transfer performance. Incorporating su into the av model reduced the absolute average deviation (AAD) from 13.9% to 9.6%. Similarly, the incorporation of su into various mass transfer models has been shown to enhance their predictive accuracy. Finally, the mass transfer mechanism of CO2 absorption by amine solutions was explored, demonstrating that the process is controlled by reaction kinetics. These findings not only highlight the critical role of ineffective porosity in mass transfer modeling but also provide methodological guidance for the precise design, process optimization, and industrial application of packed columns.
keywords: Packed column; Mass transfer; Absorption; Ineffective porosity

author: Wu, Huizhe; Luo, Cong; Yang, Penghui; Shi, Zhaowei; Zhang, Liqi; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zheng, Ying
title: A novel mold-based granulation of calcium-based heat carriers with high uniformity and wear resistance: Method proposal and process optimization
abstract_note: As a high-energy-density heat storage technology, calcium-looping heat storage is limited in its application and promotion due to the poor mechanical properties of calcium-based heat carrier materials, which are prone to wear, breakage, and even elutriation in reactors. This work proposed a new granulation method for calciumbased heat carriers, where mold kneading was used to replace the spheronization step in the extrusionspheronization method. This not only improved pellets uniformity and sphericity but also ensured good mechanical strength and heat storage performance of the pellets. Simultaneous thermal analysis (STA), fieldemission scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), particle strength testing, and N2 isothermal adsorption-desorption were used to test the pellets prepared by this method in terms of pore structure, heat storage and release performance, mechanical strength, and other aspects. The results indicated that the pellets prepared by the extrusion-rounding method exhibited excellent mechanical properties, benefiting from their smooth surface and high sphericity derived from integral forming. Specifically, the rotational wear rate after 2000 cycles was only 1.32%; moreover, under severe calcination conditions, their heat storage density remained above 0.67 kJ/g even after 20 cycles. During the further optimization of the dry mixing process in the granulation method, it was found that changes in the ball-to-powder ratio affected cold welding issues during the ball milling process and exerted a significant impact on the performance of heat carriers. These findings indicated that the extrusion-rounding method was an efficient granulation technology, and the prepared heat carriers exhibited excellent heat storage and release performance, showing great application potential.
keywords: Thermochemical heat storage; CaO-based material; Calcium-looping; Extrusion-rounding; Granulation method

author: Han, Ziqiang; Luo, Cong; Li, Xiaoshan; Du, Zhen; Zhang, Zewu; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Enhancing CO2 capture performance of K2CO3 derived from KHCO3 precursor via dispersion on non-porous SiO2
abstract_note: Potassium-based solid adsorbents have shown considerable potential for post-combustion CO2 capture. However, their performance is often constrained by the agglomeration of the active component during preparation and adsorption processes. To address this challenge, this study proposes a strategy employing KHCO3 as the precursor dispersed on the non-porous SiO2 support to mitigate active component agglomeration and enhance CO2 capture performance. The resulting adsorbents were systematically characterized and evaluated under simulated postcombustion conditions. Results reveal that SiO2 acts as an effective dispersive support, enabling exceptionally high active phase loadings up to 70 wt%, far exceeding the optimal loadings typically reported in the literature. The KHCO3-derived adsorbents exhibit superior performance, achieving near-complete active component utilization and a maximum CO2 capacity of 4.21 mmol/g at 60 wt% loading, significantly outperforming K2CO3derived counterparts. Structural analyses confirm that this enhanced performance originates from the porous structure formed during the thermal decomposition of KHCO3. Furthermore, no crystalline K-Si byproducts or hydrated phases were detected during the CO2 adsorption process, contributing to outstanding cyclic stability with maintained structural integrity after 20 cycles. This work highlights the importance of coordinated support and precursor selection for advancing potassium-based CO2 capture materials.
keywords: Cyclic stability; CO 2 capture; Dispersed K 2 CO 3; KHCO 3 precursor; Potassium-based adsorbent; SiO2

author: Yang, Long; Zhang, Zewu; Zha, Xiaojian; Luo, Cong; Chen, Wen; Li, Xiaoshan; Li, Feixiang; Zhang, Liqi
title: Experimental study on the co-combustion characteristics and Cl/N pollutants emissions of the organic waste liquid blended with pulverized coal
abstract_note: In order to realize the harmless and resourceful treatment for organic waste liquid (OWL) incineration, the co-firing of the chlorine/nitrogen containing OWL to enhance the combustion performance and control Cl/N pollutants emissions is proposed in this study. And the effects of key operational parameters (i.e., the coal types, blending coal ratios (BCR), oxygen concentrations, and reaction temperatures) on the pollutant emissions characteristics are systematically investigated. Results demonstrate that the co-firing of OWL with Wucaiwan (WCW) coal emits the lowest concentrations of HCl, NO, and N2O. Specifically, compared with Datong coal, the peak values of HCl and N2O emissions reduce by 70.0% and 92.0%, respectively, and NO emissions decrease by 55.2%. At an optimal BCR of 0.5, HCl emissions per unit mass of OWL are reduced by 70.5%. As oxygen concentration increases from 15% to 35%, NO emissions are increased but HCl formation is suppressed, while the burnout rate consistently remains > 98%. When the reaction temperature increases from 800 degrees C to 1300 degrees C, HCl formation is over 900 degrees C and reaches the peak at 1100 degrees C. NO emissions are decreased by 84.1% and the blended fuel burnout is improved. In a whole, under optimized operational conditions (e.g., BCR = 0.5, reaction temperature = 1000 degrees C), the co-firing of OWL blended with WCW coal is regarded as an effective technical route for minimizing Cl/N pollutants emissions.
keywords: Combustion performance; Co-firing; HCl emission; NOx emission; Organic waste liquid incineration

author: Hou, Dingmingyang; Luo, Cong; Du, Zhen; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Calcium-based pellets prepared by coal fly ash and cement Co-supporting method for CO2 capture
abstract_note: Calcium-based sorbents are plagued by sintering during calcium looping (CaL) cycles, which severely degrades their CO2 capture performance. To address this issue, this study proposed a novel co-supporting method for preparing calcium-based sorbent pellets. The modified sorbents were fabricated by blending Ca(OH)2, coal fly ash, and cement, followed by extruding the slurry into pellets. The performance of sorbent pellets was systematically evaluated. The results demonstrated that both coal fly ash and cement exerted a positive effect on enhancing the CO2 capture performance. Specifically, the formulation containing 10 wt% coal fly ash and 15 wt % cement exhibited the optimal comprehensive performance among all the samples: it achieved a cumulative CO2 capture capacity of 6.03 g CO2/g sorbent over 20 cycles. Additionally, this formulation retained 64.7 % of its initial CO2 capture capacity after 20 cycles, which was significantly higher than the 47.2 % retention rate of the pure CaO sorbents (100Ca). Furthermore, this modified formulation exhibited a compressive force of 69.12 N. This study revealed that coal fly ash effectively enhanced the initial carbonation conversion, while cement improved the sorbents' cyclic stability and mechanical strength. Moreover, characterization results elucidated the distinct roles of the two additives: coal fly ash functioned as an inert support, preserving the sorbents' pore structure and mitigating particle agglomeration; cement reacted with CaO to form Ca12Al14O33-an inert skeleton that supported the pore structure, thus effectively suppressing sintering.
keywords: CO2 capture; Calcium-based sorbents; Cement; Coal fly ash; Extrusion method

author: Jiang, Wufeng; Zhang, Liqi; Zhang, Yindi; Li, Xiaoshan; Chen, Wen
title: Effect of SO2 on CO2 absorption in the biphasic [N1111][Gly]/ethanol absorbent: Experimental evaluation and quantum chemical analysis
abstract_note: Biphasic absorbents employing ionic liquids (ILs) possess the potential to reduce the energy needed for regeneration, making them a promising alternative for post-combustion CO2 capture. Given that SO2 is a typical impurity in flue gas and shares similar acidic properties with CO2, it is essential to understand the effect of SO2 on CO2 capture by ILs-based biphasic absorbents. However, the majority of current research on CO2 biphasic absorbents has focused on pure CO2 or CO2/N2 mixed gas, with limited investigation into the absorption and mechanism at SO2 and CO2 coexistence. This study investigated the impact of 2000 ppm SO2 on the CO2 capture performance of a biphasic absorbent ([N1111][Gly]/ethanol). The interaction between SO2 and CO2 during the absorption process was analyzed in combination with experiments and quantum chemical calculations. The pathway for SO2-product formation and the mechanism of competition between SO2 and CO2 absorption were elucidated. In the presence of 2000 ppm SO2, the saturated CO2 loading of [N1111][Gly]/ethanol absorbent was considerably decreased, with a 65% reduction in CO2 loading after 5 cycles. Meanwhile, CO2 had a relatively minor effect on SO2 absorption. Structural characterization and theoretical calculation results indicated that [N1111][Gly] absorbed SO2 to form a thermal stable amino sulfinic ester, which was difficulty in desorption. Therefore, the absorbent's cycling performance was irreversibly degraded. SO2 competed with CO2 for amino sites, exhibiting a stronger site-locking effect that weakened the CO2 interaction with the reaction site, reducing CO2 loading.
keywords: Carbon dioxide capture; Biphasic absorbent; Glycinate ionic liquids; Competitive mechanism; Sulfur dioxide effect

author: Lu, Bowen; Fu, Aijun; Han, Ziqiang; Zhang, Liqi; Wang, Chunbo
title: Enhanced In-Zr Composite through Zr Addition for Reverse Water-Gas Shift Reaction via Oxygen Vacancies
abstract_note: Indium oxide is widely applied in CO2 hydrogenation for its excellent performance. However, In2O3 undergoes rapid deactivation during hydrogenation processes. Here, In2O3-ZrO2 composites with different nIn:nZr mole ratios are synthesized by a deposition-precipitation method, followed by a 500 degrees C calcination process, and the CO2 hydrogenation performance in converting CO2 to CO has been investigated. XRD, BET, TEM, and H2-TPR analyses were carried out to explore the physicochemical properties of these In-Zr composite catalysts. CO2 performance results imply that combining In2O3 with ZrO2 significantly enhances the CO production rate. Among these In-Zr composite catalysts, the 7In3ZrO x composite catalysts exhibits the best CO2 conversion, achieving a 40% yield of CO. In contrast, an excessively high Zr content leads to extensive ZrO2 coverage on the surface, which diminishes the CO2 conversion activity. The introduction of Zr into the In-Zr composite catalyst facilitates oxygen vacancy formation, resulting in a significantly higher CO yield compared with pure In2O3 (ca. 6%). Oxygen vacancies on 7In3ZrO x enhance CO2 hydrogenation to generate CO under reaction conditions. Furthermore, Zr addition enhances the In-O-Zr interaction and improves the stability of the In-Zr composite in the RWGS reaction.

author: Yang, Penghui; Luo, Cong; Wu, Huizhe; Du, Zhen; Liu, Bo; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Zewu; Zhang, Liqi
title: Ball milling assisted Al/Si co-doping for enhanced performance of calcium-based CO2 adsorbents: Structural evolution and synergistic mechanisms
abstract_note: The rapid sintering and performance degradation of calcium-based adsorbents during cyclic CO2 capture represent a major bottleneck limiting their large-scale application. While ball milling combined with inert oxide doping is an effective anti-sintering strategy, most studies focus on single-oxide doping systems using high-purity materials. In industrial applications, cost-effective Al2O3 and SiO2-rich minerals or composite supports are commonly used as dopant sources. However, the synergistic mechanism between ball milling and such multi-component co-doping remains poorly understood, hindering the rational design of high-performance adsorbents. To address this, we investigate the synergistic modification mechanism of ball milling with Al2O3 and SiO2 co-doping. The results indicated that the synergistically modified sample (80Ca-10Al/10Si) exhibited excellent sintering-resistant stability, retaining a CO2 capture capacity remaining at 0.36 g CO2/g adsorbent after 50 cycles. And ball milling achieved nanoscale homogeneous dispersion of Al and Si components within the CaO matrix. This subsequently facilitated the in-situ formation of an interwoven composite inert skeleton consisting of Ca3Al2O6 and CaSiO3 during cycling. This structure effectively pinned the CaO grain boundaries, suppressing high-temperature sintering. Concurrently, the modification increased the surface oxygen vacancy concentration, specific surface area and pore volume of the adsorbent. This change reduced the activation energy of the fast reaction stage by more than 40%, and consequently, a marked improvement in reaction kinetics. This work elucidates the synergistic mechanism of ball milling and multi-component doping in constructing a sintering-resistant nanocomposite structure, providing a new approach for designing calcium-based CO2 adsorbents with high stability and reactivity.
keywords: CO2 capture; Calcium looping (CaL); Cyclic stability; Ball milling; Al/Si doping; Sintering resistance

However, the synergistic mechanism between ball milling and such multi-component co-doping remains poorly understood, hindering the rational design of high-performance adsorbents. To address this, we investigate the synergistic modification mechanism of ball milling with Al2O3 and SiO2 co-doping. The results indicated that the synergistically modified sample (80Ca-10Al/10Si) exhibited excellent sintering-resistant stability, retaining a CO2 capture capacity remaining at 0.36 g CO2/g adsorbent after 50 cycles. And ball milling achieved nanoscale homogeneous dispersion of Al and Si components within the CaO matrix. This subsequently facilitated the in-situ formation of an interwoven composite inert skeleton consisting of Ca3Al2O6 and CaSiO3 during cycling. This structure effectively pinned the CaO grain boundaries, suppressing high-temperature sintering. Concurrently, the modification increased the surface oxygen vacancy concentration, specific surface area and pore volume of the adsorbent. This change reduced the activation energy of the fast reaction stage by more than 40%, and consequently, a marked improvement in reaction kinetics. This work elucidates the synergistic mechanism of ball milling and multi-component doping in constructing a sintering-resistant nanocomposite structure, providing a new approach for designing calcium-based CO2 adsorbents with high stability and reactivity.

author: Zha, Xiaojian; Zhang, Zewu; Lai, Guanfei; Zhao, Zhenghong; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: An improved kinetic model of NOx formation in NH3/coal co-combustion: Experimental and chemical kinetic study
abstract_note: High NOx emissions represent one of the key bottlenecks constraining the development of coal/NH3 co-combustion technology. Existing NOx prediction models predominantly focus on pure coal or NH3-related gas-phase combustion, lacking comprehensive consideration of the interaction behavior between coal and NH3, as well as NO reduced by char heterogeneous reaction. To systematically elucidate the fuel-N conversion during coal/NH3 co-firing, this study innovatively integrates detailed NH3 oxidization mechanisms and C-N interaction reactions into a NOx prediction model for coal combustion, establishing a comprehensive nitrogen chemistry model for coal/NH3 co-combustion, which has been validated through the co-combustion experiments based on the entrained flow reactor under different conditions. Results show that, under high NH3 blending ratio and fuel-lean conditions (NBR = 40% and alpha = 1.3), the prediction error of NO emissions decreased from 106% to similar to 2.5% after using the HHPG mechanism to replace the original coal combustion mechanism. The HNO oxidation reactions are significantly stronger than the release of vol-N, becoming the main contributor to NO formation. When alpha increases from 1.0 to 1.3, the enriched O free radical promotes the conversion of NH2 to HNO and NH to NO, accelerating the NO formation. When the temperature increases from 1273 K to 1473 K, the ROP values of R721 and R722 increase by 41 times and 18 times, respectively. The increased temperature promotes the conversion of NH2 to HNO, and the increased free radical pool concentration accelerates the oxidation of HNO, producing more NO. The new mechanism elucidates the fuel-N conversion pathways during coal/NH3 co-firing and provides theoretical foundations for subsequently developing a simplified mechanism applicable to CFD simulations.
keywords: Pulverized coal; Kinetic analysis; Ammonia; Co-firing; Fuel-N conversion pathways

author: Yang, Penghui; Chen, Junyu; Luo, Cong; Wan, Jiayao; Du, Zhen; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Fragmentation mechanism of CaO-based CO2 adsorbent pellets stored in moist air and its anti-fragmentation strategy via hydrothermal Shell-Core structuring
abstract_note: Calcium-based adsorbents hold great promise for large-scale CO2 capture. However, CaO pellets suffer fragmentation when stored in moist air, limiting their application. Numerous studies demonstrate that the presence of water significantly affects the structural integrity of adsorbent pellets. However, most of these studies focus on high-temperature conditions, while limited attention to moist air conditions. This study investigated the effect of different hydration pathways on the structural integrity of CaO pellets and revealed the fragmentation mechanism under moist air. Multiscale characterizations showed that CaO pellets exposed to moist air or liquid water underwent severe fragmentation, primarily caused by liquid-water hydration reactions. FT-IR and TGA results confirmed the presence of residual water in the fragmented pellets, while Rietveld refinement showed a significant drop in crystallinity and rise in microstrain. These changes compromised the structural integrity of pellets. In contrast, the pellets undergoing high-temperature steam hydration retained structural integrity, showing high crystallinity and low microstrain. Based on this, a hydrothermal shell-core strategy was proposed to enhance the anti-fragmentation performance of CaO pellets. A dense Ca(OH)2 shell formed via high-temperature steam hydration isolated the CaO core from moist air, significantly enhancing structural integrity. Pellets with over 60 % hydration conversion retained 74.07 % structural integrity after 48 h of exposure to moist air (30 degrees C and 85 % RH), three times higher than unhydrated pellets, while CO2 capture capacity of the revised pellets surpassed 0.6 g CO2/g adsorbent. These findings provide both mechanistic basis and design strategy for the production, transportation, and storage of calcium-based adsorbent pellets in moist environments.
keywords: CO2 capture; Calcium-based adsorbent; High-temperature steam hydration; Moist air; Pellet fragmentation; Shell-Core structure

author: Xu, Bin; Gao, Xiaoyi; Jiang, Wufeng; Tang, Xi; Li, Xiaoshan; Luo, Cong; Wu, Fan; Chen, Wen; Zhang, Liqi
title: Desorption energy analysis and assessment for CO2-rich amine solution regeneration: a mass transfer and equilibrium approach
abstract_note: The energy-intensive regeneration process of amine solutions is the main obstacle for the CO2 capture process. Direct measurement of energy consumption during the rich amine solution regeneration process is often constrained by high labor demands and operational costs, limiting its scalability in industrial applications. To address these challenges, this study proposes an innovative framework for predicting the desorption energy consumption (Qreg) based on mass transfer kinetics and gas-liquid equilibrium theory, offering a viable alternative for optimizing energy usage in CO2 capture systems. Emphasizing practical relevance, the research systematically evaluates Qreg under varying operational conditions and decomposes it into absorption heat (Qabs), sensible heat (Qsen), and latent heat (Qlat) components within a stripper. Experimental analysis reveals that, in addition to the intrinsic thermodynamic properties of the absorbents, key operational parameters, including liquid flow rate (L), lean and rich CO2 loadings (alpha leanand alpha rich), and absorbent concentration (C), have a substantial impact on gas-liquid equilibrium and mass transfer, and thus on Qreg. By strategically optimizing these parameters within a sustainable operating window, significant reductions in Qreg can be achieved. A novel predictive framework based on gas-liquid equilibrium and mass transfer kinetics was developed to accurately predict Qsenand Qlat, achieving average absolute deviations (AAD) of 11.9% and 9.5%, respectively. This approach not only enhances predictive capability but also contributes to the development of energy-efficient CO2 capture technologies.
keywords: CO 2 capture; Stripper; Absorbent; Operational parameters; Regeneration energy

author: Zha, Xiaojian; Zhang, Zewu; Lai, Guanfei; Zhao, Zhenghong; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: The development of skeletal mechanism for NOx formation in the pulverized coal/NH3 co-combustion with wide NH3 blending ratios
abstract_note: To elucidate the NOx formation mechanism in coal/NH3 co-combustion, this study developed a new comprehensive chemical reaction mechanism by coupling coal combustion mechanism and NH3 combustion mechanisms. The optimal co-combustion mechanism (HHPG-mech) is selected from eight coupled mechanisms through entrained flow combustion experiments validation. Based on this, the minimal skeletal mechanism (Ske-mech3) of the HHPG-mech is constructed by using a multi-step mechanism reduction method. Results indicate that, among eight coupled mechanisms, the HHPG-mech has the best overall prediction performance, especially for NO emission. As NBR increases from 0 % to 30 %, the NO emission of HHPG-mech predicted and Ske-mech3 predicted increases from 160 ppm to 565 ppm and from 161 ppm to 571 ppm, respectively, with a maximum error below 5 %. There are three main NO formation routes in coal/NH3 co-combustion, in which the NH2-* HNO-* NO route has an unassailable dominance. Under all NBR conditions, The ROP values of R141 (HNO + H=NO+H2) and R142 (HNO + O=NO+OH) are significantly higher than other NO production reactions, indicating that the oxidization of HNO is the key to NO formation. In addition, under low XO2 conditions, the O radical is the key to rapidly promoting NO production via enhancing reaction R142.
keywords: Pulverized coal; Co-combustion; Chemical kinetic analysis; NH3

author: Luo, Junwei; Yang, Long; Zhang, Zewu; Li, Feixiang; Zha, Xiaojian; Jiang, Qiaoyun; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: Experimental and kinetic study on pyrolysis and combustion characteristics of sugar tar waste liquid (STWL): Effects of water contents and O 2 concentrations
abstract_note: In this study, the pyrolysis and combustion characteristics of sugar tar waste liquid (STWL) affected by different water contents and oxygen concentrations are studied by using a Thermogravimetric analysis, and the kinetic parameters of the pyrolysis and combustion are obtained by the Coats-Redfern integral method. The results show that both the pyrolysis process and the combustion process of the STWLs are divided into two stages under the different water contents and oxygen concentrations. The low and high temperature ranges for pyrolysis and combustion process are below 420 degrees C and 420 -500 degrees C and below 400 degrees C and 400-500 degrees C, respectively. As the water contents increase, the pyrolysis initial temperature Ti,p gradually decreases and the comprehensive pyrolysis characteristic index D also decreases for the pyrolysis process. The Ti,p increases from 224 degrees C to 350 degrees C, and the index D decreases from 3.30 x 10-4 to 0.811 x 10-4 %3<middle dot>min-2 <middle dot> degrees C-3 . The combustion ignition temperature Ti,c increases and the comprehensive combustion characteristic index S decreases. When the oxygen concentration increases, the ignition temperature and the burnout temperature remain almost constant, with variations of 3.6% and 2.0%, respectively. Besides, the pyrolysis and combustion process of the STWL obeys the stochastic nucleation and subsequent growth model, i.e., [- ln(1-alpha)]4. These results are expected to provide some valuable guidance for organic waste liquid incineration treatment. keywords: Combustion characteristics; Incineration treatment; Kinetic analysis; Organic waste liquid; Water and O (2) content

author: Mao, Wenchao; Zhang, Zewu; Zha, Xiaojian; Dong, Jia'ning; Le, Xiaoyu; Li, Xiaoshan; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: Numerical study of reaction behavior affected by the aspect ratio and atmospheres for a single rod-shaped biomass char particle during oxy-fuel combustion
abstract_note: In pulverized biomass oxy-fuel combustion systems, biomass particles typically exist in rod-shaped forms with varied aspect ratios. Such particles exhibit differential mass transfer characteristics, which makes the particle reaction behavior unclear. This study aims to investigate the reaction behavior of rod-shaped biomass char particles with different aspect ratios during oxy-fuel combustion under different O-2 and H2O concentrations. The results show that the surface temperature and reaction distribution of the rod-shaped particle exhibit significant inhomogeneity, which intensifies as the aspect ratio increases. As the particle aspect ratio increases, the homogeneous reaction rate significantly decreases, the maximum decrease in R2 reaches 58.66 %, and the sensitivity to the aspect ratio follows the order of R2 (CO + H2O + 0.5O(2) -> CO2 + H2O) > R3 (CO2 + H-2 -> H2O + CO) > R1 (CO + H2O -> CO2 + H-2). The high aspect ratio inhibits the char oxidation and gasification process, and the inhibiting effect is more significant for the gasification reactions. As the aspect ratio increases from 1 to 3.5, the values of Da(s,gas) decreases by 30.4 % and Da(s,ox) increases by 20.4 %. Additionally, low-concentration H2O (2 %) significantly enhances particle surface temperature and homogeneous reaction rates. Homogeneous reaction rates follow the order of R2 > R1 > R3 at X-O2 = 10 %, but shift to R2 > R3 > R1 at X-O2 = 15 % and 21 %. As the H2O concentration increases, the gasification reaction shifts to diffusion control, while the oxidation reaction is gradually controlled by kinetics.
keywords: Oxy-fuel combustion; Reaction behavior; Aspect ratios; H 2 O and O 2 concentrations; Pulverized biomass

author: Fan, Yu; Lu, Bowen; Shen, Sheng; Luo, Cong; Li, Xiaoshan; Chen, Wen; Zhang, Liqi
title: The interface-induced synergistic mechanism: enhanced CO2 capture and in-situ hydrogenation on CaO-Cu/CeO2 dual-functional materials
abstract_note: The rational design of efficient and stable dual-function materials (DFMs) represents a pivotal factor in advancing the practical application of the ICCU-RWGS process. Owing to their excellent hydrogenation performance and relatively mild active temperature range, Cu-based catalytic components hold promising prospects in the ICCURWGS process. In our previous work, the application of Cu-based catalytic components in the RWGS reaction was systematically discussed, and the CaO-Cu/CeO2 DFMs with different support morphologies were designed and synthesized. However, the mechanisms underlying how Cu loading regulates the in-situ hydrogenation performance and cyclic stability of CaO-Cu/CeO2 DFMs, as well as the synergistic enhancement mechanism within the Ca-Cu-Ce system, still require further clarification. In this work, CaO-xCu/CeO2 DFMs with different Cu loading were synthesized using a solution combustion method coupled with physical mixing. Characterization revealed that a reasonable Cu loading amount can effectively optimized the oxygen vacancy concentration at the Cu-Ce interface and enhanced the ion transport between the active metal and the support. The framework structure of the xCu/CeO2 catalytic component provides sufficient physical support, effectively improving the cyclic stability of CaO-xCu/CeO2 DFM. Benefiting from the clarified synergistic enhancement mechanism of Ca-Cu-Ce system, the CaO-5Cu/CeO2 DFM exhibits optimal ICCU-RWGS performance, achieving a CO2 capacity of 14.93 mmol/g and a CO yield of 10.22 mmol/g, and retains a CO yield exceeding 85 % of its initial value following 6 h cyclic reactions. This study aims to propose an optimization strategy for the design of DFMs in the ICCU-RWGS process based on the interfacial synergistic effect.
keywords: CaO-Cu/CeO2; Dual-functional materials (DFMs); Integrated CO 2 capture and utilization (ICCU); Reverse water gas shift (RWGS)

author: Zhao, Zhenghong; Shi, Songwei; Zhao, Jiayu; Zhang, Zewu; Zha, Xiaojian; Zhang, Liqi
title: Heterogeneous reaction behaviors and NOx emission in coal MILD combustion under various primary/secondary air injection conditions
abstract_note: In coal MILD combustion process, char gasification is strengthened under high-temperature and intense-dilution environment, complicating its interaction with char oxidation and significantly affecting fuel-N migration. This work numerically investigates the effects of incident velocity and separation distance on coal MILD combustion, particularly focusing on revealing the inherent association between heterogeneous reaction behaviors and NOx emission under various primary/secondary air injection conditions. The results show that the in-furnace flue gas recirculation is enhanced to inhibit temperature fluctuation under high-velocity injection, while the enlarged separation distance delays the fuel-oxidizer jet mixing and decelerates the overall combustion rate. They both facilitate the expansion of MILD combustion region dominated by diffusion/kinetics (0.1 < Dat,max < 0.6). In general, char oxidation is within diffusion/kinetics-controlled regime, while char gasification is within kineticscontrolled regime. As incident velocity increases, the turbulent transport of reactant mixture is weakened, promoting the diffusion effect on char oxidation and reducing its contribution on char consumption. Meanwhile, the kinetic effect for char gasification is promoted and its contribution on char is increased significantly. By contrast, the enlargement in separation distance has a relatively minor effect on heterogeneous reactions. The overall NOx emission level is reduced with the increased incident velocity and enlarged separation distance, resulting from the inhibited formation of fuel-NOx and strengthened NOx-reburning. The positive linear correlation between strong gasification and low-NOx emission is revealed, providing new insights into NOx controlling based on the optimization of initial primary/secondary air injection conditions in coal MILD combustion.
keywords: MILD combustion; Char gasification; Incident velocity; NO x emission; Separation distance

author: Luo, Tong; Luo, Cong; Du, Zhen; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Controllable fabrication of high-performance porous cage-like CaO microspheres by dual-fluid spray combustion synthesis for thermochemical energy storage
abstract_note: Calcium-Looping (CaL) technology offers a promising thermochemical energy storage solution for concentrated solar power plants, leveraging the reversible carbonation/calcination reactions of CaO. To address the sintering-induced degradation in cyclic performance, this study introduces a novel dual-fluid spray combustion (SC) synthesis to fabricate porous cage-like CaO microspheres with enhanced energy storage/release performance. A systematic investigation was conducted to evaluate the effects of atomization oxygen flow rate, solution concentration, molar ratio of citric acid to Ca2+ (CA/Ca2+ ratio) and furnace temperature on combustion characteristics, cyclic performance, and microstructural properties (particle size distribution, micromorphology, crystallite size, pore size distribution, and specific surface area). Results reveal that increasing atomization oxygen flow rate from 6 to 10 L & sdot;min-1 inducing unstable combustion that refines crystallite size (73-54 nm) and particle size (62.29-39.89 mu m) alongside increased surface area (16.82-24.38 m2 & sdot;g-1) and ultimately improving 30 cycles' accumulated energy storage density (AESD30, 32.52-38.56 kJ & sdot;g-1). Conversely, elevated solution concentration or CA/Ca2+ ratio increases liquid viscosity, leading to unstable combustion and larger particles, yet smaller crystallite sizes due to reduced flame temperature. Lower CA/Ca2+ ratio conditions yield CaO microspheres with lower initial yet more stable performance, achieving an AESD30 of 40.73 kJ & sdot;g-1 for the sample prepared at 0.5 mol & sdot;L-1, though posing challenges in recovering ultrafine particles. Furnace temperature in 500-700 degrees C range shows negligible impact on cyclic performance, whereas 800 degrees C exacerbates sintering. Correlation analysis highlights average particle size (dave) as a critical determinant: smaller dave improves initial carbonation performance but accelerate cyclic decay, while larger crystallite size and mesoporous volume fraction sustain post-cycle performance.
keywords: Thermochemical energy storage; Calcium looping; Dual-fluid atomization; Porous microspheres; Spray combustion

author: Xu, Jingjing; Cao, Xinhe; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan
title: Prospects of Magnesium-Based Adsorbents in Pre-Combustion CO2 Capture: A Review from Laboratory Preparation to Scale-Up Application Exploration
abstract_note: Effective capture and resource utilization of industrial carbon dioxide has become a key strategy in addressing global climate challenges. Medium-temperature solid adsorbents have emerged as a key research area for the coordinated development of carbon capture and hydrogen energy industries due to their thermodynamic compatibility and process integration potential with clean hydrogen production methods such as steam methanol reforming and biomass pyrolysis. Among medium-temperature adsorbents, MgO-based adsorbents, with their thermodynamic stability and tunability, are the primary application materials for precombustion carbon dioxide capture under medium-temperature conditions. Although multivariate modification strategies-including metal/nonmetal doping, surfactant-mediated pore structure optimization, molten salt promoters and amine functionalization-have significantly enhanced CO2 adsorption capacity and cyclic stability, these materials have yet to be scaled for industrial application. Persistent challenges include kinetic limitations of adsorbents, inadequate mechanical stability and coupling issues with multicomponent industrial gas environments. This review systematically explores the relationship between the structure and performance of magnesium oxide adsorbents, emphasizing microscopic regulation strategies such as defect engineering, mesoporous architecture construction and interfacial modification to reinforce adsorption mechanisms. Furthermore, it addresses key engineering approaches aligned with industrial requirements, including reactor design optimization, operational parameter tuning and antipoisoning process development. By integrating materials design with process innovation, this work provides theoretical insights to accelerate the transition of medium-temperature carbon capture technologies from laboratory research to large-scale deployment, thereby supporting carbon neutrality objectives.

author: Yang, Long; Zhang, Zewu; Li, Feixiang; Zha, Xiaojian; Mao, Wenchao; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: New insights on co-combustion of organic waste liquid blending with pulverized coal: Synergistic effect on the combustion performance and pollutant control
abstract_note: Organic waste liquid (OWL) poses a significant threat to water resources and human health. Therefore, clean and efficient approaches for OWL treatment have gained widespread attention. In this study, the co-combustion of OWL blended with pulverized coal (PC) is investigated. The combustion characteristics and gas-phase products are analyzed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS). The results show that the pyrolysis of OWL occurs in two stages, while both PC and OWL/PC pyrolysis follow three stages. The co-pyrolysis of OWL and PC promotes the release of volatile compounds. Compared to PC pyrolysis, the pyrolysis release index (D) of OWL/PC increases by 420 %. Additionally, co-pyrolysis helps suppress chlorine release from OWL, with chlorides comprising 15.7 % of the gas-phase products for OWL, compared to only 0.5 % for OWL/PC. Compared to OWL combustion, the ignition and burnout temperatures of OWL/PC are reduced by 30.8 K and 72.7 K, respectively. Overall, the co-pyrolysis and co-combustion of OWL and PC enhance OWL combustion intensity and reduce pollutant emissions. These findings provide valuable insights for the efficient and clean treatment of OWL.
keywords: Combustion performance; Co-firing; Gas-phase products; Organic waste liquid; Pollutants

author: Yang, Penghui; Luo, Cong; Du, Zhen; Shi, Zhaowei; Wu, Huizhe; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Enhanced CO2 capture capacity and mechanical stability of calcium-based pellets by dry ball milling of lime to extrusion molding
abstract_note: The low-cost granulation of calcium-based adsorbent is crucial for its application in large-scale CO2 capture. To prepare calcium-based pellets with high CO2 capture capacity and mechanical stability, a dry ball milling to extrusion molding method was proposed using slaked lime and cordierite mixture. The key effects of ball-to-powder ratio, ball milling duration and ball milling speed on the characteristics, CO2 capture performance, as well as mechanical strength of the pellets were investigated in depth. The results demonstrated that slaked lime and cordierite mixture was a good precursor for granulation dry ball milling to extrusion molding method. All three factors exerted significant influence on the adsorbent. Specifically, the ball-to-powder ratio had the greatest impact on the CO2 capture capacity, while ball milling duration affected the mechanical performance. The optimal pellets exhibited the initial mechanical strength of 47.3 N and achieve a CO2 adsorption capacity of 0.34 g/g after 50 cycles. Besides, the effective ball milling could reduce the volume-weighted mean particle size of the adsorbent powder to one-ninth of its original size, resulted in the increase of surface area and pore volume of the adsorbent, while prolonged ball milling could result in cold welding and agglomeration of the adsorbent. Moreover, mechanical analysis revealed that the dispersion of compressive force during the initial carbonation/ calcination cycle became more concentrated, with the standard deviation decreased from 16.9 N to 5.6 N, due to the high ball-to-powder ratio and extended ball milling duration. But the effects were weakened with the increase of cycle number.
keywords: CO 2 capture; Ball milling; Calcium-based adsorbent; Mechanical strength; Pellets

author: Zhu, Zhengyang; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: Investigations on the thermal properties and thermal adaptability of binary double-peak phase change materials for battery thermal management
abstract_note: Phase change material (PCM) cooling technology, characterised by a simple system, low cost, and low energy consumption, is regarded as a promising approach for battery thermal management systems (BTMS). However, the application of the conventional single-peak PCMs has been limited due to the narrow melting temperature range and poor adaptability to variable ambient conditions. To address this limitation, this study proposes a strategy to improve the adaptability of BTMS by using binary double-peak phase change materials with a wide transformation temperature range. The thermal storage characteristics of double-peak PCMs were systematically evaluated. Results show that the first endothermic peak originates from the melting of the eutectic structure, whereas the second corresponds to the melting of the remaining component. An enthalpy prediction model was established, achieving a prediction error of less than 5 % across three representative series. Thermal simulations demonstrated that double-peak PCMs offers improved adaptability to varying thermal generation rates and ambient temperatures. Compared to conventional single-peak PCMs, double-peak PCMs more effectively regulated battery temperature, maintaining it below 60 degrees C even under extreme ambient conditions (50 degrees C). These findings indicate that the proposed strategy enhances BTMS adaptability and has strong potential for practical application.
keywords: Thermal management; Enthalpy prediction model; Fatty acid; Phase change material; Schrader equation; Thermal simulation

author: Zha, Xiaojian; Zhang, Zewu; Zhao, Zhenghong; Yang, Long; Mao, Wenchao; Lai, Guanfei; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: Numerical study of the combustion behaviors and NOx formation in NH3/coal co-combustion under MILD mode: Effect of NH3 injection modes
abstract_note: Co-combustion of pulverized coal with NH3 is a crucial strategy for achieving a low-carbon transition in the coal-fired power plant, while unstable combustion and high NOx emissions are the key technical challenges for its widespread application. The Moderate or Intense Low-oxygen Dilution (MILD) combustion technology attracts increasing attention due to its advantages of stable flame and low NOx emissions, thus, MILD combustion technology is applied to coal/NH3 co-combustion that can overcome the challenges. This work systematically studies the effect of five different NH3 injection modes on combustion behaviors and NOx emissions in coal/NH3 co-combustion under MILD mode. Compared with other injection modes, the center-in mode has a higher share of the MILD regime in the furnace (similar to 90 %) and a lower temperature fluctuation (4.18 %), which further improves the temperature uniformity and enhances the combustion stability. The char average burnout rate of center-in mode increases by 2.56 times compared to the regular premixed injection mode. The order of overall NO emission concentration under the different NH3 injection modes is Inner injection >> Center-out injection > Mixed injection approximate to Outer injection > Center-in injection. Compared to the original Mixed injection mode, the NO-sum concentration under the Center-in injection mode is decreased by 33.8 %. The Center-in injection mode balances well the high char burnout and low NOx emissions, which provides theoretical references for the design of new low-NOx burners for the co-combustion of NH3 and coal.
keywords: MILD combustion; NO x emissions; Ammonia; Co-combustion; Injection modes

author: Dong, Jia'ning; Zhang, Zewu; Mao, Wenchao; Le, Xiaoyu; Jiang, Qiaoyun; Li, Xiaoshan; Li, Feixiang; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: Investigation on the effects of the secondary air distribution on the flow and combustion characteristics of black liquor: A computational fluid dynamic (CFD) approach modified by the user defined functions (UDFs)
publication_title: INTERNATIONAL COMMUNICATIONS IN HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
abstract_note: To accurately predict black liquor combustion process (i.e., drying, devolatilization and char burning) in a Kraft recovery boiler, a computational fluid dynamic (CFD) approach modified by the user defined functions (UDFs) that is programed by C language is developed. Then it is embedded into FLUENT software to predict the variation of the black liquor particle diameter during the combustion process, the predicted result is nearly consistent with that of the experiment. Further, the modified CFD model is applied to the combustion simulation of a Kraft recovery boiler, and the relative deviations between the simulated and the measured values of key parameters are less than 5 %. Moreover, two different distributed modes (i.e., cross-counter, tangential) for secondary air are adopted to further suggest an improved choice for this Kraft recovery boiler. Compared with the tangential arrangement, the cross-counter arrangement mode is favor to create the swirl flow with a smaller radius in the furnace, resulting in more uniform temperature and species distribution, demonstrating more obvious advantages in reducing coking and slagging.
keywords: Black liquor combustion; CFD simulation; Kraft recovery boiler; Secondary air arrangements; UDFs
journal: INTERNATIONAL COMMUNICATIONS IN HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER

author: Xu, Bin; Gao, Xiaoyi; Jiang, Wufeng; Tang, Xi; Li, Xiaoshan; Luo, Cong; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Revisiting the role of solution aggregation in CO2 desorption: Insights of mass transfer and energy consumption in phase-change absorbents
abstract_note: High regeneration energy consumption remains the primary bottleneck hindering the large-scale industrial application of CO2 capture via chemical absorption. Recently, phase-change absorbents for CO2 capture have garnered significant research interest due to their promising potential in reducing energy consumption. Since CO2 products accumulate in the rich phase after absorption, it is widely believed that a higher degree of solution aggregation (reduced solution volume and increased CO2 product concentration) can significantly enhance the desorption process. However, we present a different conclusion: the relationship between solution aggregation and the desorption process does not follow such a simple trend. This discrepancy may arise because most studies are conducted in batch reactors (such as bubble reactors), where the gas-liquid mass transfer behavior differs fundamentally from that in industrial-scale continuous strippers. Furthermore, previous assessments of solution aggregation on regeneration often overlook the importance of achieving a lower lean CO2 loading (alpha(lean)) to sustain the overall CO2 capture capacity. To address these gaps, we conducted stripper experiments and developed a mass transfer model. The results revealed that lower liquid flow reduces the gas-liquid contact area, impeding the mass transfer of the desorption process. Higher CO2 concentration increases viscosity, reducing contact area and diffusion coefficient, and hindering desorption. An energy consumption analysis confirms that liquid flow rate (L) effects are complex, and simply reducing flow does not necessarily lower desorption energy consumption.
keywords: CO2; Energy consumption; Mass transfer; Phase-change absorbent; Stripper

author: Zha, Xiaojian; Zhang, Zewu; Zhao, Zhenghong; Yang, Long; Mao, Wenchao; Wu, Fan; Li, Xiaoshan; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: Effect of H2O physicochemical properties on NOx formation mechanism in NH3 oxy-steam combustion under MILD mode
abstract_note: Ammonia (NH3) is garnering significant attention as a renewable and sustainable zero-carbon fuel, with the potential to substitute fossil fuels in power generation systems, while its low reactivity and high NOx emissions hinder further application. MILD oxy-steam combustion, a novel combustion strategy with high combustion stabilities and low NOx emissions through coupling MILD combustion and oxy-steam (O-2/H2O) combustion, is proposed to overcome the technical bottlenecks of NH3 combustion. High H2O concentration is the key to achieving stable and low-NOx combustion in this new combustion mode. This study investigates the effect of H2O physicochemical properties on the combustion characteristics and the NOx formation mechanisms in pure NH3 oxy-steam combustion under MILD mode. Results show that high H2O concentration reduces the flame peak temperature (by 36 K similar to 99 K) and creates a more uniform temperature distribution, facilitating NH3 to achieve the MILD combustion state. Additionally, the H2O chemical effect significantly reduces NO emission compared with NO2 and N2O, with the greatest reduction of 55.8 %. The H2O chemical effect promotes the conversion of more NH2 radical into NH (NH2 + OH <-> NH + H2O) radical rather than into HNO intermediate (NH2 + O <-> HNO + H) via reducing O radical concentration and increasing OH radical concentration in the radical pool, significantly weakening the relative contribution of the route NH2 -> HNO -> NO in NO formation. This study is anticipated to offer serval valuable theoretical references for the future industrial applications of NH3 fuel.
keywords: MILD combustion; Ammonia; NOx formation mechanism; Oxy-steam combustion

author: Le, Xiaoyu; Zhang, Zewu; Zha, Xiaojian; Mao, Wenchao; Luo, Cong; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Effects of tertiary air injection on the reaction behavior and NOx emissions during pulverized coal air-staged MILD combustion
abstract_note: The combination of air-staged combustion with coal MILD combustion has the potential advantage to further reduce NOx emissions, and the reduction effect is highly affected by the tertiary air injection parameters. In this study, the influence of tertiary air injection position (zter) and tertiary air proportion (Pter) on the reaction behavior and NOx formation during pulverized coal air-staged MILD combustion are studied. Results show that, the momentum introduced by tertiary air leads to the flue gas recirculation (FGR) broken into small eddies for Pter >= 10 %. Compared with non-air-staged combustion, the average temperatures at different zter increase by 1-3 K when Pter = 5 %, while they gradually decrease by 4-7 K when Pter increases to 20 %. The MILD regime region distribution identified by internal recirculation and temperature decreases by 31.2-44.0 % with the increment in Pter from 5 % to 20 %, indicating the inhabitation to FGR from strong tertiary air. While in terms of the maximum surface Damkhler number, the MILD combustion is enhanced for Pter = 5-15 %, as the maximum surface Damkhler numbers in these cases are lower than that of the non-air-staged case. The char burnout region is expanded in air-staged combustion due to the entrainment of tertiary air to the fuel. In air-staged MILD combustion, the NO concentration in dry flue gas reaches minimum value of 168 ppm for zter = 1.0 m and Pter = 10 %, which ascribed to the large oxy-lean region where NO is converted to N2 through NH3 reduction reaction of NH3 + NO -> N2 + product 4.
keywords: MILD combustion; NOx emissions; Air-staged combustion; Combustion regime; Injection parameters

author: Cao, Dingshan; Qin, Zhen; Luo, Cong; Li, Xiaoshan; Shi, Zhaowei; Wu, Fan; Zheng, Ying; Zhang, Liqi
title: Dry reforming of methane by La2NiO4 perovskite oxide: Cordierite-ceramic-supporting monolithic catalyst with Cu B-site substitution improving reacting performance
abstract_note: This study is the extension of Dry reforming of methane by La2NiO4 perovskite oxide: B-site substitution improving reactivity and stability. The perovskite-type La2Ni1-xCuxO4 powder, which was La2NiO4 being partially substituted with Cu at the B-site, was an excellent Ni-based dry reforming (DRM) catalyst in lab-scale experiment. However, the powder should be loaded on molding materials before scale-up application to avoid inefficient heat and mass transfer in the fixed reactor, and to cut down carbon deposition. In this study, a La2NixCu1-xO4-loaded cordierite ceramic monolithic catalyst was proposed. The DRM reaction performance of the monolithic catalyst was tested on a fixed bed, and the monolithic catalyst was analyzed for pre- and postreaction characterization by tools such as XRD, BET, SEM, and Raman spectroscopy. In addition, the DRM reaction process was simulated using the computational fluid dynamics (CFD) method. The results showed that the introduction of cordierite ceramic carriers had better carbon deposition resistance while maintaining a high level of CH4/CO2 conversion compared with the powder samples. Among the La2Ni1-xCuxO4 (x = 0, 0.2, 0.4) catalytic materials with different Ni/Cu loading, La2Ni0.8Cu0.2O4 possessed a better pore structure and combined a high conversion (CH4/CO2 up to 91 %/95 %, respectively) with a low carbon accumulation rate (only 0.4 mg/(g*h)) over a sustained 120-h reaction. The CFD results showed that the reaction tubes had a relatively uniform gas flow rate in the ceramic pores and a negligible pressure drop before and after the catalyst, and there existed only one low-temperature zone with a maximum temperature difference of 17.5 K.
keywords: Cordierite; Dry reforming; La 2 Ni x Cu 1-x O 4 perovskite; Monolithic catalyst

author: Xu, Bin; Gao, Xiaoyi; Jiang, Wufeng; Tang, Xi; Li, Xiaoshan; Luo, Cong; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Novel mass transfer kinetics model for predicting phase separation behavior in CO2 capture with energy-saving biphasic absorbents
abstract_note: Biphasic absorbents exhibit excellent efficiency and significant energy-efficient potential in carbon capture. Nevertheless, in industrial applications, phase separation during CO2 absorption inside packed columns leads to increased viscosity, complicating the assessment of both mass transfer and phase separation behavior. This work introduces an innovative mass transfer kinetic model to predict phase separation and mass transfer performance of CO2 absorption into biphasic absorbents. The study examines how operational parameters influence phase separation, revealing that raising the liquid feed temperature (T), elevating the CO2 partial pressure (PCO2), increasing the CO2 loading of the lean solution (alpha lean), and lowering the liquid flow rate (L) all promote phase separation. In contrast, the inert gas flow rate (G) has little effect on phase behavior. Once phase separation occurs, the viscosity rise hinders mass transfer, causing a greater pressure drop (Delta P), higher liquid holdup (HL), and lower gas flooding velocity (uf). Ultimately, a novel mass transfer kinetic model was formulated to predict phase separation conditions. Experimental validation demonstrates that this model achieves an absolute average deviation (AAD) of only 7.95 %, which would greatly facilitate the screening and evaluation of biphasic absorbents.
keywords: CO2; Biphasic absorbents; Mass transfer kinetics; Packed column
abstract: Biphasic absorbents exhibit excellent efficiency and significant energy-efficient potential in carbon capture. The study examines how operational parameters influence phase separation, revealing that raising the liquid feed temperature (T), elevating the CO2 partial pressure (PCO2), increasing the CO2 loading of the lean solution (alpha lean), and lowering the liquid flow rate (L) all promote phase separation. In contrast, the inert gas flow rate (G) has little effect on phase behavior. Once phase separation occurs, the viscosity rise hinders mass transfer, causing a greater pressure drop (Delta P), higher liquid holdup (HL), and lower gas flooding velocity (uf). Ultimately, a novel mass transfer kinetic model was formulated to predict phase separation conditions. Experimental validation demonstrates that this model achieves an absolute average deviation (AAD) of only 7.95 %, which would greatly facilitate the screening and evaluation of biphasic absorbents.

author: Gao, Xiaoyi; Xu, Bin; Jiang, Wufeng; Tang, Xi; Li, Muyi; Wu, Fan; Luo, Cong; Li, Xiaoshan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Phase separation prediction modeling and structure-activity relationship of CO2 capture by biphasic amine absorbents based on phase separation load
abstract_note: The design and development of biphasic amine absorbents depend heavily on extensive experimental screening. This underscores the need for systematic phase separation predictive models to streamline the process and minimize trial-and-error efforts. The concept of phase separation load is proposed in this paper to evaluate the ability of biphasic amine absorbents to undergo phase separation during CO2 capture, providing a quantitative measure to assess their separation performance. Based on the phase separation loads, an analysis of the structure-activity relationships of the phase separation agents is developed. The advantages of ketone, tertiary amine, lipid, furan, and ether solvents in the manufacture of biphasic absorbents are elucidated. These advantages are attributed to the symmetrical structure of their functional groups and the absence of hydrogen bond donors. Similarly, the benefits of alcohol and amide solvents in the fabrication of non-aqueous homogeneous absorbents are clarified, attributed to the presence of hydrogen bond donors (hydroxyl group (-OH) and primary/secondary amino groups (-NH)) and the high polarity of the molecular surface. Analysis of the specific structure-activity relationships for alcohols reveals two patterns in their phase separation ability: (1) it increases with the number of CHn groups; and (2) it follows the trend: ortho-alcohols<iso-alcohols<tert-alcohols. Experimental and computational were combined to develop linear and nonlinear phase separation prediction model. Among them, the prediction equations constructed through inverse and quadratic functions exhibited the highest adjusted R-2, explaining 73.2% of the variance in phase separation load. Analysis of the fitted models reveals that the capacity of a phase separation agent to generate H-bonds, acting as a donor, is more important than polarity in dictating the phase separation behavior of biphasic absorbents.
keywords: Biphasic absorbent; Phase separation mechanism; Prediction model; Structure-activity relationships; Carbon capture

author: Wu, Huizhe; Luo, Cong; Yang, Penghui; Zhang, Liqi; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zheng, Ying
title: Microstructural evolution of tailoring CaO/CaCO3 composites with alumina particle of varied sizes for thermochemical heat storage
abstract_note: Calcium-loop (CaL) heat storage technology is a potential backup energy option for concentrated solar power plants. During repeated cycles, calcium-based materials face sintering and agglomeration, leading to a rapid decline in heat storage capacity. The most widely studied closed CO2 Brayton cycle requires calcium-based materials to undergo repeated cycles in a carbon-dioxide-rich atmosphere, but such work has been limited in the past. In this study, we synthesized various millimeter-sized calcium-based heat carrier pellets using a novel forming method and evaluated their heat storage performance. Using a simultaneous thermal analyzer (TGA/ DSC), we evaluated the materials' heat storage performance and investigated the effects of different particle-size alumina on the pore structure of calcium-based pellets through X-ray diffraction, field-emission scanning electron microscopy, N2 physical adsorption, and reaction kinetics. The study found that calcium-based pellets with large-particle-size alumina formed a web-like pore structure during cycling, exhibiting better structural stability. In contrast, those with small-particle-size better maintained their initial pore structure. Alumina-added calciumbased pellets had more abundant pores, with the large-particle-size alumina group having a specific surface area 3.5 times that of pure calcium oxide pellets. Additionally, the accumulative heat storage density of calcium-based pellets with small-particle-size alumina after 20 cycles was about 1.72 times that of pure calcium oxide pellets. It was worth noting that we found that shortening the carbonation time within the scope of this research could slow down the degradation of the exothermic performance of calcium based pellets.
keywords: Thermochemical heat storage; CaO-based material; Calcium-looping; Carbonation temperature; Evolution of morphology

author: Zha, Xiaojian; Zhang, Zewu; Zhao, Zhenghong; Yang, Long; Mao, Wenchao; Lai, Guanfei; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Liqi
title: The relationship between the moderate or intense low oxygen dilution (MILD) regime and NO formation in the co-combustion of NH3 with bituminous coal
abstract_note: NH3/coal co-combustion technology presents an effective technical pathway to rapidly and largely reducing CO2 emissions in coal-fired power plants. To mitigate the challenges of combustion instability and elevated NOx emissions in NH3/coal co-combustion, the moderate or intense low oxygen dilution (MILD) combustion technology, which has stronger stability and lower NOx emissions, is applied to NH3/coal co-combustion. To further clarify the internal relationship between the establishment of the MILD combustion regime and the reduced NOx formation, the internal recirculation distribution in the furnace is reconstructed by adjusting the injection angles (alpha) and injection distances (l) of fuel jets. The relationship between the two is then discussed from the perspectives of combustion stability, turbulence-chemistry interaction, and particle reaction behaviors. Results show that increasing alpha or l reduces the temperature fluctuation in the main reaction zone by enhancing the recirculation level (Kv) upstream of the furnace, and leads to a more stable and milder combustion status. As Kv increases, the oxidation reaction rates of volatiles and char particles decrease, and their burnout time is extended. The combustion behaviors of char shift from being Cchar-O2 dominated to being Cchar-O2 and Cchar-H2O/ CO2 co-dominated, and the consumption mode of NH3 changes from being NH3-O2 dominated to being NH3-O2 and NH3-NO co-dominated. When alpha increases to +10 degrees, the NO emission at the furnace outlet is about 860 ppm at 40 cal% NH3 blending ratio, which is 40.2 % less than that in the initial burner arrangement with alpha of 0 degrees. With the enhancement of the MILD regime, the char gasification and NH3 reduction reactions both increase and the non-oxidizing behaviors of coal and NH3 are greatly promoted, thus preventing the oxidation of fuel-N.
keywords: NO x emissions; MILD regime; combustion behaviors; NH 3 /coal co-combustion

author: Luo, Tong; Houding, Mingyang; Luo, Cong; Zhang, Yi; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Liqi
title: Porous hollow spherical CaO heat carriers for thermochemical energy storage prepared by spray pyrolysis based on a dual-fluid atomization method
abstract_note: This study presents a dual-fluid atomization spray pyrolysis (SP) synthesis to fabricate porous hollow spherical CaO heat carriers for thermochemical energy storage. Key preparation conditions, including solution concentration, molar ratio of citric acid to Ca2+ (CA/Ca2+ ratio), and pyrolysis temperature were systematically analyzed for their effects on cyclic energy storage/release performance, particle size, micromorphology, crystallite size and pore structure via simultaneous thermal analyzer (STA), field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and N2 isothermal adsorption-desorption methods. Results indicate that higher solution concentrations and CA/Ca2+ ratios increased particle size and promoted surface grains coalescence. Concentration >= 2 mol center dot L-1 or CA/Ca2+ ratio <= 0.5:1 degraded cyclic performance, and the optimal values are 1 mol center dot L-1 and 1:1. Pyrolysis temperature significantly influenced the microstructure by influencing the pyrolysis mechanism: At 400 degrees C, spray drying dominated, forming hollow spheres with uncoalesced surface grains. At 500-600 degrees C, mesoporous structure formed with enhanced grain coalescence. At 700-800 degrees C, violent pyrolysis produced irregular, macroporous particles with high initial but rapidly declining performance. The optimal temperature at 600 degrees C yielded CaO heat carriers with a high specific surface area of 33.24 m2 center dot g-1, achieving 92.8 % initial carbonation conversion and a 30-cycle accumulative energy storage density of 46.13 kJ center dot g-1, which is 2.33 times higher than commercial CaO. Correlation analysis identified the specific surface area, total pore volume, and mesoporous volume fraction as key factors influencing post-cycling carbonation conversion and accumulated energy storage density. This work demonstrates SP synthesis as a scalable approach to tailor CaO microsphere for enhanced thermochemical energy storage.
keywords: Thermochemical energy storage; Calcium looping; CaO heat carriers; Dual-fluid atomization; Porous hollow spheres; Spray pyrolysis

author: Lai, Donglong; Luo, Cong; Wang, Zhiping; Shi, Zhaowei; Luo, Tong; Zhang, Liqi; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Zewu
title: Process simulation and techno-economic analysis of CO2 capture by coupling calcium looping with concentrated solar power in coal-fired power plant
abstract_note: The calcium looping (CaL) cycle is an effective high-temperature CO2 capture technology, its primary energy consumption arises from the calcination process, which limits the development of CaL technology. Utilizing concentrated solar power (CSP) instead of combustion for the calcination process in the CaL cycle can significantly reduce the energy penalty associated with CO2 capture of coal-fired power plants. Currently, the application of coupling CaL with concentrated solar power (CaL-CSP) systems in coal-fired power plants is still in its infancy, and there is limited research on its thermal performance and techno-economic analysis. In this study, a process simulation model of a 660 MW supercritical coal-fired power plant is developed as a reference. Subsequently, four different heat transfer routes are integrated into the design of CaL-CSP systems. The results indicate that the coupled power plant achieves a CO2 capture rate of 90 % compared to the reference coal-fired power plant, and reduces annual CO2 emissions by 93 % to only 58.2 g CO2/kWh. The CO2 capture energy penalty is 0.24 MJ/kg CO2. A sensitivity analysis of power generation cost reveals that if the cost of solar concentrator components is halved, the power generation cost will decrease to just 25 % higher than the reference power plant. Given the rapid advancements in CSP technology, this reduction is likely to be realized soon. Moreover, with the increase in carbon taxes and carbon prices, the power generation cost is expected to further decrease.
keywords: CO2 capture; Calcium looping cycle; Exergy analysis; Process simulation; Techno-economic analysis

author: Shi, Zhaowei; Luo, Cong; Lai, Donglong; Luo, Tong; Yang, Penghui; Li, Xiaoshan; Wu, Fan; Zhang, Zewu; Zhang, Liqi
title: A modified pore evolution particle model applied to CaCO3 decomposition and CaO sintering during calcium looping CO2 cycles
abstract_note: The calcium looping of calcium-based carbonation/calcination cyclic reaction is an efficient decarbonization technology that can be applied to post-combustion carbon capture, solar thermal storage, and enhanced methane reforming hydrogen. The pore structure generated during the calcination stage greatly affects adsorption performance. Hence, the change of pore structure is crucial during the calcination. Existing models related to pore structure still need to be further improved regarding pore formation mechanisms. In this paper, a modified pore evolution model is proposed. The modified pore evolution model contains the kinetic equation, the CO2 diffusion equation, and the vacancy aggregates equation. The modified model considers the possibility of spontaneous pore formation in the mechanism, which can better fit the experimental results and provide higher simulation accuracy. The model reveals the diffusion of CO2 within the particles during decomposition, and spatiotemporal evolutionary properties of pore parameters. The pore change trend is the most obvious when the initial pores are mainly 3 nm mesopores. The pore evolution is relatively stable when the initial pores are mainly 25 nm mesopores. Besides, the particles experience significant pore migration during the sintering stage when the temperature reaches 1273 K. At 1073 K, the maximum pore size occurring during pore evolution did not exceed 40 nm. The model estimates a maximum pore size of more than 60 nm at 1273 K. The modified model can aid in a deeper comprehension of how the pore structure changes throughout the calcination process.
keywords: CO2 capture; Calcium looping; CaCO3 decomposition; CaO sintering; Pore model

author: Tang, Xi; Gao, Ge; Jiang, Wufeng; Xu, Bin; Wu, Fan; Luo, Cong; Li, Xiaoshan; Zhang, Liqi
title: The structure-activity relationship for CO2 absorption heat of chain diamines: Effects of amino types, methyl/ethyl groups, and chain lengths
abstract_note: Chain diamines, exhibiting favorable CO2 capture properties, including a large absorption capacity and a high absorption rate, have been extensively utilized in CO2 capture technology. It has been demonstrated that the molecular structure of chain diamines has an impact on CO2 absorption properties. The importance of absorption heat as a main component of regeneration energy consumption can not be ignored. However, the influence of the chain diamine molecular structure on CO2 absorption heat remained unclear. In this study, twelve chain di- amines were examined to investigate their structure-activity relationship with CO2 absorption heat. The results indicated that chain diamines with a high percentage of secondary or tertiary amino groups had a low CO2 absorption heat. Chain diamines with methyl groups on tertiary amino groups demonstrated a diminished CO2 absorption heat than those with ethyl groups. Compared to amino types and methyl/ethyl groups, chain lengths had a slight effect on the CO2 absorption heat of chain diamines. Chain diamines containing two carbon atoms exhibited a slightly low CO2 absorption heat. Furthermore, it was observed that TMDEA, with two tertiary amino groups, exhibited a high CO2 equilibrium solubility (0.98 mol CO2 /mol amine) and a low CO2 absorption heat (57.52 kJ/mol). The findings of this work are of significant relevance for the screening and development of low energy-consuming chain diamines suitable for CO2 capture industrial applications.
keywords: CO2 capture; Quantum chemical calculation; Chain diamines; CO2 absorption heat; Structure-activity relationships
